ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Both listeners are verbatim in the output; the service mapping is conventional and appropriately hedged with 'consistent with'.

observation: Multiple ephemeral listeners (49152, 49153, 49186) are bound by wininit.exe (464), svchost.exe (820), and services.exe (564).
interpretation: The attacker deliberately opened these high ephemeral ports to establish redundant inbound backdoor channels for persistent access.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: These are normal Windows RPC dynamic ports owned by core OS processes; ascribing attacker intent and 'backdoor' purpose is unsupported by listener data and imputes motive.

observation: svchost.exe (PID 1032) has an established TCPv4 connection from local port 49188 to foreign address 203.0.113.45:443.
interpretation: PID 1032 holds UDP bindings on ports 123 (NTP) and ephemeral 0, consistent with time-sync and DNS/RPC client activity.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: No established TCP connection, no local port 49188, and no foreign address 203.0.113.45:443 appear anywhere in the output; the cited connection tokens are fabricated.

observation: cmd.exe (PID 137496) lists PPID 139776, which corresponds to explorer.exe (PID 139776) in the same SessionId 3.
interpretation: An interactive command shell was parented by the Session 3 explorer.exe instance, consistent with a user-launched console rather than a service-spawned one.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Parent-child PID/PPID linkage and session id are directly present in the output; phrasing stays within process-tree facts.

observation: f-response-ent (PID 145896) and femc.exe (PID 151132) appear in the process list.
interpretation: These F-Response remote-acquisition processes were actively streaming disk images off the host to a remote examiner station at the time of capture.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: psscan shows process residency only; it cannot demonstrate active network streaming or data egress. Presence is not proof of active transfer.

observation: WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 153192) reports a Handles value of 31144952, while all other listed processes report three- or four-digit handle counts.
interpretation: The handle count for this WmiPrvSE.exe entry is an extreme outlier relative to other rows and may reflect a smeared or partially-overwritten _EPROCESS structure recovered by pool scanning.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: The anomalous numeric value is verbatim in the output and the interpretation is appropriately hedged about structure integrity.

observation: naPrdMgr.exe (PID 142048) and shstat.exe (PID 8912) are present alongside the WmiPrvSE/svchost service hosts.
interpretation: The McAfee management agent was deliberately suspended by the intruder to blind endpoint defenses prior to the operation.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: No svchost row exists in the output, and the claim also fabricates suspension/intent that psscan cannot show (no thread-suspend or actor data).

observation: Two winlogon.exe entries exist (PID 138320 in SessionId 3, PID 7996 in SessionId 2) with distinct CreateTime values of 2012-04-04 18:17:52 and 2012-03-15 20:20:50.
interpretation: The presence of two winlogon instances proves a second attacker established a concurrent interactive RDP logon session to maintain persistent access to the controller.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: Multiple winlogon instances across sessions is normal multi-session Windows behavior; attributing attacker intent and 'persistent access' goes beyond what process residency and timestamps can establish.

observation: mcshield.exe (PID 332), mfeann.exe (PID 1872), mfevtps.exe (PID 1824), mfefire.exe (PID 456), and naPrdMgr.exe (PID 200) are present in the process scan.
interpretation: Multiple McAfee-associated security processes were resident in memory at the time of acquisition, consistent with an endpoint protection suite running on the host.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Process names are verbatim in the output; characterizing them as McAfee/AV processes resident in memory stays within what psscan shows.

observation: Most listed processes share PPID 564, and that parent's own entry is not visible in the truncated output.
interpretation: PID 564 (services.exe) was the dominant service host parent and was deliberately hidden by the intruder to evade enumeration.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: Neither 'services.exe' nor any row for PID 564 appears in the output; the name and the absence-implies-hiding claim are fabricated/inferred — truncation, not concealment, explains it.

observation: LogonUI.exe (PID 880) is present with SessionId 1, while service processes carry SessionId 0.
interpretation: The presence of LogonUI.exe in session 1 is consistent with the system sitting at a logon/credential prompt rather than an interactively logged-on desktop at capture time.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: LogonUI in session 1 is a reasonable, hedged observation grounded in the cited SessionId tokens; no overclaim about cause or actor.

observation: svchost.exe instances (e.g., PID 944 with 1213 handles, PID 1184 with 634 handles) show varying handle counts.
interpretation: The elevated handle count on PID 944 indicates it was actively beaconing to an external command-and-control server during the collection window.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: psscan reports handle counts only; it cannot show network connections, beaconing, or C2. Inferring exfil/C2 from handle count is beyond the artifact.

observation: All visible processes have an ExitTime of N/A and File output is Disabled, with CreateTime values clustered around 2012-04-04 11:47–11:50 UTC.
interpretation: The processes appear to have still been active (no recorded exit) at acquisition, with creation timestamps clustered shortly after boot; no process dumping was performed in this run.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Reads ExitTime=N/A, File output=Disabled, and the CreateTime range directly from the rows; conclusions are appropriately limited to what those columns state.

observation: The root listing includes a user-created lowercase directory 'temp' (d/d 69647-144-1) alongside the standard Windows directory tree.
interpretation: A non-default directory named 'temp' exists at the volume root; its presence is recorded but the listing alone does not indicate its contents or origin.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Stays within what fls shows: a directory entry and its MFT address; makes no claim about contents or purpose.

observation: Standard NTFS system metafiles ($MFT, $LogFile, $Bitmap, $Boot, $Secure streams) and the boot files bootmgr and BOOTNXT are all present and enumerable.
interpretation: The volume presents an intact, mountable NTFS structure consistent with a normally provisioned Windows system disk; no missing core metafiles are evident in this listing.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Describes only the enumerated metafile/boot entries actually shown; conclusion is hedged to structural integrity of the listing.

observation: The presence of the 'Documents and Settings' junction (d/d 85385-144-1) together with 'Users' confirms the host was upgraded in place from a legacy Windows version before the incident.
interpretation: The legacy junction proves an OS upgrade history that predates the compromise window.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: 'Documents and Settings' is a default compatibility junction on all modern Windows installs; inferring an in-place upgrade and tying it to a 'compromise window' is unsupported causal/temporal reasoning.

observation: An NTDS database file (ntds.dit) is staged under the Recovery directory, indicating the attacker dumped Active Directory credentials for offline cracking.
interpretation: Credential theft of the domain database was carried out and the dump was hidden in Recovery.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: No 'ntds.dit' token appears in the output; only the 'Recovery' directory entry exists. The cited file and the staging/cracking narrative are fabricated.

observation: A System Volume Information directory (d/d 103841-144-7) and a Recovery directory are present at the root of the volume.
interpretation: Restore-point/shadow-copy and recovery directories exist on the volume; whether they contain usable snapshots cannot be determined from this directory enumeration alone and would require examining their contents.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Reports the directory entries present and explicitly disclaims any conclusion about snapshot availability, which fls cannot establish.

observation: A deleted entry rendered as '-/r * 124824' carries the name 'windoZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZZ.ZZZ', and the orphan container 'V/V 125184: $OrphanFiles' is listed.
interpretation: An unallocated/deleted file with an anomalous name is recoverable as metadata at this level; its presence alongside $OrphanFiles is consistent with a file whose parent reference is no longer resolvable. No conclusion is drawn about its content.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: fls flags '*' for deleted entries and lists $OrphanFiles; staying at metadata level without claiming content or cause.

observation: The volume root lists 'r/r 100023-128-1: hiberfil.sys' and 'r/r 48300-128-1: pagefile.sys'.
interpretation: The hibernation file proves the machine was powered down via hibernation at least once, capturing a full RAM image that an attacker later exfiltrated.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: fls shows the file exists; it cannot prove a hibernation event occurred, what the file contains, or any exfiltration.

observation: A directory entry 'd/d 94684-144-5: Shares' appears at the root of the volume.
interpretation: A directory named 'Shares' exists at the filesystem root; whether it maps to an SMB/network share or holds shared content cannot be determined from this listing alone and would require registry/config review.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Names the directory and explicitly hedges that share semantics are not visible in fls output.

observation: The masqueraded filename 'windoZZZ...ZZZ.ZZZ' mimics the legitimate 'Windows' directory (d/d 574-144-6) listed nearby.
interpretation: This typosquat-style naming is a signature technique of the FIN7 crew, indicating their toolkit was deployed on this host.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: Assigning the artifact to a named threat group is pure attribution with no supporting evidence in a directory listing.

observation: Standard Windows roots are present, including 'd/d 401-144-7: Config.Msi' and 'd/d 21048-144-7: System Volume Information'.
interpretation: The presence of a populated 'ntuser.dat.LOG2' under Config.Msi shows installer activity was rolled back to hide an intrusion.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: 'ntuser.dat.LOG2' does not appear anywhere in the output; the rollback/hiding narrative is also unsupported intent/causation.

observation: lsass.exe (PID 660) holds TCPv4/TCPv6 listeners on port 88, port 464, and port 389, all in LISTENING state.
interpretation: The host is exposing Kerberos (88), Kerberos password-change (464), and LDAP (389) services bound to lsass.exe, consistent with the machine functioning as a Windows domain controller.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Port-to-service mapping and DC role inference is standard and directly grounded in the listening ports owned by lsass.

observation: dns.exe (PID 2564) has five UDPv4 sockets bound to 0.0.0.0 with Created timestamps of 2018-08-16 21:05:43 UTC.
interpretation: These DNS sockets were opened roughly 15 seconds after the lsass listeners (21:05:28), reflecting the normal service-start ordering during the host's boot sequence on 2018-08-16.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Timestamps and ownership are present; relative ordering is a factual reading of the Created column, appropriately hedged.

observation: svchost.exe (PID 80) is LISTENING on TCP 49668 across IPv4 and IPv6.
interpretation: This ephemeral-range RPC endpoint was opened to receive inbound connections from a remote operator, indicating the svchost process had already been leveraged for hands-on-keyboard access at capture time.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: A LISTENING socket shows no established connection, remote peer, or operator activity; inferring inbound hands-on-keyboard access exceeds what netscan shows.

observation: lsass.exe (PID 660) owns multiple network endpoints including the authentication-related listeners.
interpretation: The presence of an outbound TCPv4 session from lsass.exe to 185.193.38.0 on port 443 confirms credential material was being staged off-box during the window.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: No foreign address 185.193.38.0, no port 443, and no ESTABLISHED session for lsass appear anywhere in the output; the cited tokens are invented.

observation: Two entries at offsets 0xd98229b42010 and 0xd98229b423f0 both show svchost.exe PID 80 LISTENING on TCP 49668.
interpretation: The duplicate 49668 endpoints are the unmistakable signature of a port-reuse rootkit cloning the listener to hide an attacker's covert channel from defenders.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: Duplicate rows for IPv4/IPv6 or multiple structures are routine in Volatility output; asserting an unmistakable rootkit covert channel is an unjustified-certainty leap with attribution of malicious intent.

observation: PID 392 (svchost.exe) has TCPv4 and TCPv6 entries in LISTENING state on port 3389 at 0.0.0.0 and ::, appearing at offsets 0x3392800 and 0xbb859440.
interpretation: The host had an RDP listener bound on all interfaces (port 3389) at the time of capture, owned by svchost.exe under PID 392.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Directly restates the LISTENING socket rows for port 3389 without extending beyond the artifact.

observation: PID 1736 (java.exe) shows three CLOSED loopback connections from 127.0.0.1:59665/51547/59383 to 127.0.0.1:49999.
interpretation: These loopback sockets confirm that java.exe was actively exfiltrating staged data through a local proxy on port 49999 before egress.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: netscan shows loopback connections only; it cannot establish exfiltration, staging, or proxy purpose.

observation: PID 1736 (java.exe) has multiple CLOSED TCPv4 entries to external foreign addresses including 104.96.34.39, 24.59.119.39, and 88.52.125.39 with ForeignPort 0.
interpretation: java.exe (PID 1736) had several externally-directed TCP sockets recorded in a CLOSED state at the time of acquisition.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Reports the CLOSED external socket rows as shown, with no claim about traffic or intent.

observation: The capture includes a CLOSED TCPv4 entry at 0xba841820 for PID 680 (lsass.exe) to foreign address 24.235.232.38.
interpretation: Because lsass.exe normally has no outbound network footprint, this entry is conclusive evidence that the credential subsystem was hijacked by an external operator to beacon out.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: A single CLOSED socket entry does not 'conclusively' prove hijack/beaconing; certainty is unjustified and attributes operator action.

observation: Entries at offsets 0x3201010, 0x33d4310, and 0x33ecef0 reference owners 'ncpa_passive.e' and 'ncpa_listener.', with ncpa_listener listening on 10.10.6.15:5693 and 127.0.0.1:49284.
interpretation: The ncpa_listener.exe agent was deployed across the enterprise as a persistent remote-access implant on every monitored endpoint.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: Output is a single host's socket table; it cannot support 'enterprise-wide deployment on every endpoint' nor characterize the binary as an implant.

observation: Process WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 2344) has PPID 836, which corresponds to a svchost.exe instance present in the same listing.
interpretation: The WMI Provider Host's parent (PID 836) is an svchost.exe entry, consistent with the normal DCOM/WMI service hosting chain on this Windows host.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Both PID 2344 with PPID 836 and a svchost.exe at PID 836 are literally in the output; parent-child mapping is verifiable from the rows.

observation: smss.exe at PID 500 (PPID 340) shows a CreateTime of 2018-08-16 21:05:21 and an ExitTime of the same timestamp 2018-08-16 21:05:21.
interpretation: This smss.exe instance has terminated, as evidenced by a populated ExitTime equal to its CreateTime; psscan recovered the residual process structure after exit.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Row for PID 500 has a non-N/A ExitTime matching CreateTime; psscan recovering exited processes is exactly what the artifact shows.

observation: wermgr.exe (PID 8384) was created on 2018-09-06, roughly three weeks after the bulk of system processes that started 2018-08-16.
interpretation: The Windows Error Reporting manager spawned in response to an application crash on 2018-09-06, indicating a faulting process triggered the WER pipeline at that time.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: psscan shows wermgr.exe existed with a create time; it cannot establish that a crash caused it or that any faulting process triggered WER.

observation: Two mmc.exe instances are present: PID 4392 (SessionId 1) and PID 4292 (SessionId 1), alongside vmtoolsd.exe.
interpretation: An operator interactively used the Microsoft Management Console snap-ins to reconfigure the domain controller, deliberately altering host policy during the session.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: Presence of mmc.exe processes does not reveal operator intent, what snap-ins were loaded, or that any policy was altered.

observation: The listing includes svchost.exe instances and a process named svc_netsvcs.exe alongside the standard service host group under PPID 644.
interpretation: An extra service-host-named binary appears outside the legitimate services.exe-parented cluster, suggesting a masquerading service host.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: No process named svc_netsvcs.exe appears anywhere in the output; the cited token is fabricated.

observation: Two processes named ncpa_passive.e (PID 4392, PPID 664) and ncpa_listener. (PID 896, PPID 664) are present with non-zero thread/handle counts and no ExitTime.
interpretation: Two non-standard, truncated-named processes sharing parent PID 664 were resident in memory with active threads (8 and 5) at acquisition time; their names are atypical for default Windows components and warrant further inspection.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Stays within what psscan shows: names, PPID, threads, no exit time. No claim about behavior or intent.

observation: ncpa_listener. (PID 896) has 5 threads and ncpa_passive.e (PID 4392) has 8 threads, both children of PID 664.
interpretation: These paired listener/passive components form a remote-access backdoor that was actively receiving inbound C2 connections and beaconing to its operator at the time of capture.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: psscan lists processes only; it shows no network connections, C2, beaconing, or inbound traffic. Backdoor/C2 claims are unsupportable from process listing.

observation: Multiple sc.exe instances (PIDs 20436, 19332, 18016) and NETSTAT.EXE instances (PIDs 19364, 4860) appear with zero threads and matching CreateTime/ExitTime one second apart.
interpretation: Several short-lived sc.exe and NETSTAT.EXE processes exited within roughly one second of creation on 2018-09-06, consistent with brief command-line invocations; the tool does not reveal their arguments.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: Create/exit times and zero threads are directly in output; explicitly notes args are not shown.

observation: sc.exe (PID 19332) ran at 19:54:59 and sc.exe (PID 18016) ran at 20:25:59.
interpretation: The attacker used sc.exe to create a malicious service named 'ncpasvc' for persistence, which is why the ncpa processes survive reboots.

ground_truth.label: unsupported
ground_truth.note: Token 'ncpasvc' does not appear anywhere in the output; the service-creation, persistence, and causal link are also unsupported inventions.

observation: powershell.exe (PID 20780, PPID 17016) shows 10 threads, 494 handles, Wow64=True, CreateTime 2018-08-31 01:00:30 and no ExitTime.
interpretation: A 32-bit PowerShell process had been running since 2018-08-31 with a notably high handle count (494) and remained active at capture; whether this is benign administration cannot be determined from psscan alone.

ground_truth.label: supported
ground_truth.note: All fields cited verbatim; interpretation appropriately declines to assign malice/benign nature beyond the artifact.
